1: 'I have made a covenant with my eyes; how then could I gaze at a virgin?' Chapter 31 is the ethical centerpiece of the entire book — the most comprehensive inventory of moral responsibility in the OT. The oath of purgation (šəḇûʿaṯ bərāʾ) was a recognized legal form in the ancient Near East: the accused could swear an elaborate self-imprecatory oath to establish innocence when no witnesses were available. The covenant with his eyes against gazing at a virgin is the first clause — the control of desire at the point of origin (the eyes), before it issues in action. In Matthew 5:27-28, Jesus builds on exactly this tradition: the problem of lust is not merely the physical act but the covetous gaze, which Job here claims to have covenanted against.
2: 'What would be my portion from God above and my heritage from the Almighty on high?' The rhetorical question about divine retribution for violating the eye-covenant introduces the theological framework of the oath: Job appeals to divine retribution as the sanction for his oath. In ANE legal practice, oaths invoked divine powers as witnesses and enforcers — the perjurer would face the deity's punishment. The divine names ʾĒl and šadday (the Almighty) are the wisdom tradition's preferred names for the international deity who enforces moral order beyond Israel's covenant boundaries.
3: 'Is not calamity for the unrighteous, and disaster for those who work iniquity?' Job states the retribution theology not as his own view but as the theological framework within which his oath operates: the assumption is that wrong-doing brings calamity. This is the very theology the friends have applied to Job — and Job here uses it as the framework for his oath, not to endorse it unconditionally but to demonstrate that even by that framework, his suffering is inexplicable given his moral record. The tension is deliberate: if the retribution principle is true (as Job is here assuming for the oath's logic), then his suffering proves a violation of the principle.
4: 'Does he not see my ways and number all my steps?' The divine omniscience that serves as the oath's enforcement mechanism is here made explicit. In the ANE context, divine surveillance of all human action was a foundational religious assumption — the Mesopotamian sun-deity Shamash (god of justice) was believed to see all things; the Ugaritic divine assembly maintained cosmic order by observing all human action. Job appeals to this divine omniscience not as a threat but as the ground of his oath: he has nothing to hide from the God who numbers all steps (cf. Psalms 139:3: 'you search out my path and my lying down and are acquainted with all my ways').
5: 'If I have walked with falsehood and my foot has hastened to deceit.' The first formal oath clause. The conditional form (ʾim, if — followed by the self-curse in Job 31:7-8) is the standard ANE oath structure: 'if I have done X, then let Y happen to me.' The falsehood (šāwəʾ) and deceit (mirmāh) targeted here are the foundational social virtues of the honor culture — truth-speaking and straight-dealing in commerce and social relations. In the ancient marketplace and court, false dealing undermined the entire system of honor that social life depended on; Job swears he has not participated in this.
6: 'Let me be weighed in a just balance, and let God know my integrity!' The image of the balance (mōʾznayim ṣedeq, just scales) is the standard icon of justice in the ancient Near East — the Egyptian weighing of the heart scene, Mesopotamian justice imagery, and Israelite law all use the scale as the instrument of fair assessment. Job's request that God weigh him in just scales is a dramatic reversal of his earlier complaint that his calamity is too heavy for scales (Job 6:2-3: 'Oh that my vexation were weighed... it would be heavier than the sand of the sea'). Job is confident that a just divine assessment will vindicate him.
7: 'If my step has turned aside from the way and my heart has gone after my eyes, and if any spot has clung to my hands.' The triple condition expands the oath: feet turning aside from the right path (moral direction), heart following the eyes (desire leading to action), and stain on the hands (the physical consequence of wrongdoing — the same 'handwashing' imagery of innocence in Deuteronomy 21:6-7 and Psalms 26:6). In Israelite jurisprudence, the hands that did the act were literally implicated in the act's moral weight — ritual handwashing as innocence declaration and the 'blood on your hands' idiom reflect the somatic moral theory in which the body parts that commit the act bear the guilt.
8: 'Then let me sow, and another eat, and let what grows for me be rooted out.' The self-curse of losing the harvest to another is drawn directly from the Deuteronomic covenant curse structure (Deuteronomy 28:30-31, 33: 'you shall plant a vineyard but shall not eat of it... a nation you have not known shall eat up your livestock and the fruit of your ground'). The loss of one's agricultural labor to another was the covenant curse for covenant violation. Job adopts this structure as the appropriate penalty for the moral violations he is denying — if he has done these things, let the covenant curses fall on him.
9: 'If my heart has been enticed toward a woman, and I have lain in wait at my neighbor's door.' The adultery clause is the most socially and legally charged in the oath. In ancient Israelite law, adultery was a capital offense (Leviticus 20:10; Deuteronomy 22:22) and a violation of the neighbor's property rights (the wife was legally under the husband's authority). The phrase 'lying in wait at the neighbor's door' uses the vocabulary of the stalker and the ambusher — it is not a spontaneous act but a premeditated one, which increases the culpability. The wisdom literature's treatment of the adulteress's husband as a cuckolded victim (Proverbs 6:32-35: the jealous husband will show no mercy) reflects the severe social consequences of adultery for all parties.
10: 'Then let my wife grind for another, and let others bow down over her.' The self-curse for adultery is the degradation of his own wife: forced labor (grinding grain was the work of slaves — Exodus 11:5; Isaiah 47:2; Lamentations 5:13) and sexual violation by others. The talion principle (an eye for an eye) is applied to sexual sin: Job, who has (he swears) not violated his neighbor's wife, swears that if he has, his own wife may be violated. The harshness of this self-curse reflects the severity with which the ancient world treated sexual violations of household boundaries — the honor of the household was dependent on the sexual fidelity of all its members.
11: 'For that would be a heinous crime; that would be an iniquity to be punished by the judges.' The term zimmāh (heinous crime, a crime considered especially vile — used for incest in Leviticus 18:17; Job 20:14, and for other sexual violations) identifies adultery as among the most serious categories of social violation. The involvement of judges (pᵊlîlîm, the judicial authorities who governed the city gate) confirms that this is a formally cognizable offense, not merely a private moral failure. In the ancient world, the court at the city gate had jurisdiction over crimes against the household and the community; adultery was among the offenses that came before these judges.
12: 'For that would be a fire that consumes as far as Abaddon, and it would burn to the root all my increase.' The fire metaphor for adultery's destructive power uses ʾăḇaddôn (Abaddon, the place of destruction — a name for the underworld deeper than Sheol, used in Job 26:6; Job 28:22; Revelation 9:11). Fire that burns down to Abaddon is the most comprehensive destruction imaginable: it consumes not just the present estate but reaches to the very depths of existence. In Proverbs, the adulterer's fire is similarly described as self-destructive (Proverbs 6:27-29: 'can a man carry fire next to his chest and his clothes not be burned?'). The root destruction (šōrāšay tiśśārēš) removes all future possibility — not just present prosperity but generational continuation.
13: 'If I have rejected the cause of my manservant or my maidservant, when they brought a complaint against me.' The treatment of slaves is the unexpected center of Job's oath — unexpected because it was not legally required in the ancient world. In Israelite law and ANE law generally, slaves had limited legal standing: the master was not typically required to adjudicate the slave's complaint fairly or at all. The fact that Job swears he has heard the complaints of his slaves (ʿeḇed and ʾāmāh) reflects an ethical position that exceeds legal obligation. This anticipates the Torah's development of slave rights (Exodus 21:1-11; Deuteronomy 15:12-18) and points forward to the NT's treatment of the slave-master relationship in Ephesians 6:9 and Philemon.
14: 'What then shall I do when God rises up? When he makes inquiry, what shall I answer him?' The theological grounding of the slave-treatment oath is striking: Job treats his slaves fairly because of divine examination (ṣāḏāh), not legal requirement. The same divine omniscience that grounds the oath (Job 31:4: God numbers all steps) requires fair treatment of slaves: if God is watching all human interactions, the slave's cause is as visible to God as the cause of the free citizen. This is the OT's most explicit statement of the theological basis for slave rights: divine justice does not recognize the social distinctions that human law enforces.
15: 'Did not he who made me in the womb make him? And did not one God fashion us in the womb?' The common origin of master and slave in the divine creative act is the anthropological ground for their equal moral status before God. In the ancient world, the social hierarchy was assumed to be divinely ordained — the Babylonian creation myth Enuma Elish explicitly creates humanity to serve the gods and implicitly legitimates human hierarchy. Job's theological argument is the counter: the same God who fashioned Job (ʿāśānî) fashioned the slave (yaʿăśēhû). The common humanity created by a single God establishes a moral floor beneath which the powerful cannot push the powerless without violating the Creator's intent.
16: 'If I have withheld anything that the poor desired, or have caused the eyes of the widow to fail.' The turn to the poor, widow, orphan, and stranger (Job 31:16-23) addresses the prophetic social ethics that ran through the entire OT tradition. The widow's failing eyes (ʿênê ʾalmānāh ʾăkalleh) is the image of the widow who waits and watches for help that does not come — her eyes 'failing' from the effort of looking and hoping. This image appears in the lament psalms (Psalms 69:3: 'my eyes grow dim with waiting for my God') and in the Servant's ministry to the failing-eyed (Isaiah 42:7: 'to open the eyes that are blind'). Job swears he has not been the cause of that failure.
17: 'Or have eaten my morsel alone, and the fatherless has not eaten of it.' The communal table as moral obligation is a fundamental ANE social institution. In the ancient world, the generous host who fed all comers was the ideal of the nobility — the great man's table was the community's safety net. The isolation of food ('eating my morsel alone') was a recognized sign of social failure in a culture where table fellowship defined social bonds. In contrast, the ideal man's table in Proverbs 9 and in the ANE wisdom tradition is open: wisdom's house provides food for all. Job swears his table has been the traditional open table of the hospitable great man.
18: 'For from my youth the fatherless grew up with me as with a father, and from my mother's womb I led the widow.' The claim that Job cared for the orphan from his youth is one of the book's most remarkable assertions: it defines Job's character as consistently oriented toward the vulnerable from the very beginning of his adult life. In the ancient world, the care of orphans and widows was the mark of the righteous king — the Hammurabi Code opens with Hammurabi's claim to care for the widow and orphan; the Ugaritic Aqhat epic praises the father Dan-el for judging the widow's cause. Job claims this royal-patron virtue as his personal characteristic from childhood.
19: 'If I have seen anyone perish for lack of clothing, or the needy without covering.' The obligation to clothe the naked is closely related to the food obligation in ANE social ethics. The prophets identify clothing the naked as a mark of true worship alongside feeding the hungry (Isaiah 58:7: 'when you see the naked, to cover him'). In the covenant lawsuit section of Isaiah 1, the failure to care for the orphan and widow is the primary evidence of covenant violation. Job's oath that he has never seen the naked without clothing them implicitly appeals to the same prophetic standard.
20: 'And his body has not blessed me, and he was not warmed with the fleece of my sheep.' The blessing of the body (mᵊtānāyw bērăḵûnî) — literally 'his loins blessed me' — is the physical expression of gratitude from the one who has been clothed: warmth experienced in the body is the evidence that the charitable act has been real and effective. Job's insistence on this somatic evidence is consistent with the ancient body-map in which social action was experienced physically. The 'fleece of my sheep' grounds the charitable act in Job's pastoral economy — the same flocks that made him wealthy have clothed the poor.
21: 'If I have raised my hand against the orphan, because I saw my help in the gate.' The raising of the hand against the orphan (ʾip̄ṯoh yāḏî ʿal-yāṯôm) in the gate context is the legal image: the gate was the site of judicial proceedings, and raising a hand against the orphan meant using one's legal influence to oppress the powerless in the legal system. Job's specific temptation would have been to use his powerful gate-position (Job 29:7-10: the princes and elders deferred to him at the gate) to oppress those who had no legal advocate. The phrase 'because I saw my help in the gate' acknowledges the temptation: when you are powerful in the legal system, oppressing the powerless is easy and goes unpunished.
22: 'Then let my shoulder blade fall from my shoulder, and let my arm be broken from its socket.' The anatomical self-curse (shoulder blade from shoulder, arm from socket) uses the imagery of violent injury — these are wounds sustained in military combat or serious accidents. The hands and arms that would have oppressed the orphan are the specific body parts that would bear the punishment. In ANE legal tradition, the talion principle was applied to the offending body part: the hand that struck was cut off (Hammurabi Code §195); the eye that transgressed was put out. Job's self-curse adopts this talion logic — if his arm has oppressed, let his arm be destroyed.
23: 'For I was in terror of calamity from God, and I could not have faced his majesty.' The explicit motivation for Job's ethical behavior — terror of divine judgment and inability to face divine majesty (śeʾēṯô, his rising/majesty) — grounds the entire oath in theological fear rather than social convention or legal obligation. This is significant: Job's ethics are not primarily cultural conformism but theological response to divine omniscience. The same divine majesty that the friends invoke as the ground of their theology (and that Job challenges in the legal speeches) is here acknowledged as the motivating force behind Job's ethical life. Job's theology is not inconsistent — he fears divine judgment precisely because he takes it seriously.
24: 'If I have made gold my trust or called fine gold my confidence.' The wealth oath (Job 31:24-25) addresses the temptation of prosperity — specifically the temptation to trust in wealth rather than in God. In the wisdom literature, the danger of riches is well-documented (Proverbs 11:28: 'whoever trusts in his riches will fall'; Proverbs 23:4-5). The specific contrast between trusting in gold (zāhāḇ... kāṯem) and trusting in God is the prophetic critique of idolatry — in the prophets, wealth becomes a functional idol when it replaces divine dependence (Isaiah 2:7-8; Ezekiel 7:19: 'their gold and silver cannot deliver them in the day of wrath').
25: 'If I have rejoiced because my wealth was abundant or because my hand had found much.' The joy in wealth (ʾeśmaḥ kî-rāḇ ḥêlî) is what the Deuteronomic tradition warns against: 'beware lest you say in your heart, "My power and the might of my hand have gotten me this wealth"' (Deuteronomy 8:17). Job's oath that he has not rejoiced in wealth as wealth — but has held it as a trust from God — is the rejection of what the prosperity gospel would call blessing but what the wisdom tradition calls a snare. The hand that 'found much' (māṣāʾ yāḏî rāḇ) is the attribution of wealth to human agency that the Deuteronomic tradition identifies as spiritual danger.
26: 'If I have looked at the sun when it shone, or the moon moving in splendor.' The astral worship oath addresses a temptation that was particularly acute in the ancient world. The worship of the sun and moon was the dominant form of religious practice in Mesopotamia (Shamash the sun, Sin the moon) and was a recognized temptation for Israelites (Deuteronomy 4:19; Job 17:3; 2 Kings 23:5, 11; Jeremiah 8:2; Ezekiel 8:16). The magnificence of the sun and moon — their undeniable visual splendor — made them natural objects of religious awe. Job's oath that he has not responded to this splendor with religious devotion is a claim to monotheistic fidelity in an environment where astral worship was everywhere.
27: 'And my heart has been secretly enticed, and my mouth has kissed my hand.' The specific gesture of the kiss of the hand toward the heavenly body was the standard act of astral worship in the ancient world — documented in Mesopotamian, Egyptian, and Canaanite religious practice. The secrecy (bassēṯer) is important: public astral worship was legally prohibited in Israel (Deuteronomy 17:3-7), so the private act of hand-kissing toward the sun or moon was the clandestine form of the temptation. Job's oath covers not just public idolatry but private religious heart-devotion — he has not even secretly kissed his hand toward the heavenly bodies.
28: 'This also would be an iniquity to be punished by the judges, for I would have been false to God above.' The classification of astral worship as an iniquity requiring judicial punishment (ʿāwōn pᵊlîlî) is directly drawn from Deuteronomic law (Deuteronomy 17:12: the case of astral worship goes to the judges, and the penalty is death). Job's oath addresses the most serious category of Israelite religious offense — the worship of other deities — and swears that he has not committed it even in the secret of his heart. The grounding 'I would have been false to God above' (ḵiḥaštî lāʾēl mimaʿal) identifies the theological offense beneath the legal one: astral worship is lying to God.
29: 'If I have rejoiced at the ruin of him who hated me, or exulted when evil overtook him.' The enemy-joy oath (Job 31:29-30) addresses what Proverbs calls šāmaḥ bᵊmappleṯ śōnəʾeḵā (rejoicing in the fall of your enemy, Proverbs 24:17-18) — a recognized temptation that the wisdom tradition explicitly prohibits. In the honor-shame culture, the defeat of an enemy was naturally cause for satisfaction: it vindicated one's honor and demonstrated the enemy's wrong. But the wisdom tradition's prohibition reflects a deeper anthropological awareness that gloating over another person's calamity, even an enemy's, violates the common humanity that dignity requires. Job swears he has not done this.
30: 'I have not let my mouth sin by asking for his life with a curse.' The curse against the enemy (lᵊʾallēl bᵊnap̄šô, to curse his life/soul) was a recognized legal and religious act in the ancient world — formal curses were believed to be effective, and calling down divine judgment on an enemy was both a common desire and a recognized religious act. In the Psalms, the imprecatory psalms (Ps 69, 109, 137) express exactly this desire. Job swears he has not even given voice to the desire for his enemy's death — he has not prayed against them. This exceeds even the wisdom tradition's command not to rejoice at the enemy's fall; Job has not even allowed his mouth to formalize the inner desire for the enemy's destruction.
31: 'If the men of my tent have not said, "Who is there that has not been filled with his meat?"' The hospitality clause recalls the social obligation of the great man's table. In the ancient world, the great household (ʾohel, tent — a patriarchal term for the clan household) was expected to be generous to all who came. The rhetorical question 'who has not been filled with his meat?' asked by the 'men of his tent' (his household and community members) is the characteristic praise of the hospitable host: everyone testifies that no one has gone away unfed. Job's claim is that his household was known for this — the universal positive testimony of the community members is the evidence.
32: 'The stranger has not lodged in the street; I have opened my doors to the traveler.' The obligation to house strangers (gēr, the resident alien or the traveling stranger) was one of the most emphasized social obligations in Israelite law (Leviticus 19:33-34; Deuteronomy 10:18-19; Job 14:29; Job 24:17-22). The theological grounding was the Exodus: 'you shall not wrong a stranger or oppress him, for you were strangers in the land of Egypt' (Exodus 22:21). In the wider ANE context, hospitality was the sacred obligation of all civilized people — the gods themselves were believed to travel in disguise and to reward or punish the hospitable and inhospitable (reflected in Gen 18 and in the Greek tradition of Zeus Xenios, the god of hospitality). Job's open door to the traveler fulfills both social and theological obligation.
33: 'If I have concealed my transgressions as others do by hiding my iniquity in my heart.' The concealment of sin (ḵissîṯî kᵊʾāḏām pᵊšāʿāy) is contrasted with the open disclosure that Job's oath represents. The phrase 'as others do' (kᵊʾāḏām, like Adam, or like humankind) may specifically allude to Adam's hiding in the garden after the fall (Genesis 3:8-10: Adam hid himself from the face of YHWH) or may use 'Adam' as a generic reference to human concealment. Job's oath is itself the anti-concealment: by making these formal declarations in front of witnesses, he is bringing his entire moral record into public view rather than hiding it. The open oath is the formal opposite of Adam's hiding.
34: 'Because I stood in great fear of the multitude, and the contempt of families terrified me, so that I kept silence and did not go out of doors.' The social fear that could prevent honest speech — fear of the crowd (hāmôn) and family contempt (bûz mišpāḥôṯ) — is the social pressure that the honor-shame culture exerts against honest disclosure. Job is claiming that he was not silenced by social fear: he did not suppress the truth to protect his reputation before the crowd. This is significant: in the honor-shame world, social pressure was the most powerful force constraining behavior. Job's oath claims independence from this pressure in both his ethical behavior and his willingness to make this public declaration.
35: 'Oh, that I had one to hear me! Here is my signature! Let the Almighty answer me! Oh, that I had the indictment written by my adversary!' The climax of the oath — the tāw (last letter of the alphabet, the signature mark — tāwî) is Job's formal signature on the oath-document. In ancient legal culture, the completion of a legal document was marked by the signatory's seal or signature. Job signs the oath with the final letter of the alphabet — a complete signature — and demands a written indictment (sēp̄er, scroll/document) from his adversary (God, treated as the opposing party in the lawsuit). The demand for a written indictment is the formal legal move that requires the opposing party to specify their charges in writing — the most specific and committed form of accusation.
36: 'Surely I would carry it on my shoulder; I would bind it on me as a crown.' The image of wearing the adversary's indictment as a crown rather than hiding from it is an extraordinary reversal of the shame/honor dynamic. In the ancient world, an indictment was carried as a sign of shame — the accused bore the document of their accusation publicly. Job's claim that he would wear it as a crown (ʿăṭārāh, the royal or victory crown) transforms the legal document of shame into a badge of honor: the indictment's content would, if read, demonstrate his innocence and thereby function as a crown of vindication. The bravado of the image reflects Job's total confidence in his case.
37: 'I would give him an account of all my steps; like a prince I would approach him.' The 'account of all my steps' (mispār ṣəʿādāy ʾaggîḏennû) is the formal accounting that the divine book would require — every step documented and explained. Job would approach this accounting 'like a prince' (kᵊmô-nāgîd) — with the dignity and confidence of a nobleman, not the cowering of the guilty. The word nāgîd (prince, leader, designated leader) is used for the king-designate and for military commanders — it implies the bearing of someone who knows their position and their right. Job's confidence in approaching the divine accounting is the culmination of the entire oath: he has nothing to hide because he has done nothing to hide.
38: 'If my land has cried out against me and its furrows have wept together.' The land-testimony oath (Job 31:38-40) shifts from social ethics to agricultural ethics — specifically, the obligations of the landowner to the land and to those who work it. In the OT, the land itself was understood to be a moral witness: it could cry out against the blood shed on it (Genesis 4:10: Abel's blood crying from the ground), and it vomited out the inhabitants who defiled it with sexual sin (Leviticus 18:25, 28). The covenant between the Israelite people and the land was moral — the land's productivity depended on the people's faithfulness.
39: 'If I have eaten its yield without payment and made its owners breathe their last.' The specific violation addressed here — eating the land's yield without payment and causing the owners to die — refers to land theft by causing the rightful owners to die. This connects to the Naboth's vineyard narrative (1 Kgs 21): Ahab and Jezebel's seizure of Naboth's vineyard through false accusation and judicial murder is the paradigmatic case of this violation. Job swears he has not engaged in this form of social violence — using economic or judicial power to dispossess legitimate landowners. The prophets repeatedly condemn this violation (Isaiah 5:8: 'woe to those who join house to house, who add field to field').
40: 'Let thorns grow instead of wheat, and foul weeds instead of barley.' The covenant curse of agricultural reversal — thorns and weeds replacing wheat and barley — is drawn directly from the Deuteronomic curse structure (Deuteronomy 28:38-42: crop failures as covenant punishments) and echoes the fall narrative (Genesis 3:17-18: 'cursed is the ground because of you; thorns and thistles it shall bring forth for you'). The specific reversal of the cultivated crops (wheat, barley) into the uncultivated wilderness plants (thorns, weeds) represents the un-creation of agriculture — the reversal of the human calling to cultivate the earth. Job accepts this most agricultural of curses as the appropriate consequence of agricultural injustice.
